title: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Bypass
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="ics:sis"
| where match(status_message, "(?i)bypass|override|maintenance|manual|inhibit")
| lookup ot_sis_authorized_changes loop_id user OUTPUT permit_id valid_until
| eval permit_ok=if(!isnull(permit_id) AND _time<=valid_until, 1, 0)
| where permit_ok=0
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen values(status_message) as states by loop_id, asset, user
description: Monitors mode changes, jumpering, or overrides on SIS (T0800), directly tied to process safety and regulatory expectations.
value: Monitors mode changes, jumpering, or overrides on SIS (T0800), directly tied to process safety and regulatory expectations.
implementation: Use Splunk ES and OT Safety data integrations. Require MOC/permit correlation. Escalate immediately to process safety and T0800 workflows.
visualization: State timeline per SIS loop, Alert list with permit linkage, Gauge of open bypasses.
knownFalsePositives: Test and drill cycles for safety systems, and written overrides during documented hazards, can trip bypass alerts even when the plant follows procedure — we verify procedure IDs.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:sis"` safety PLC diagnostics, alarm historians.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Use Splunk ES and OT Safety data integrations. Require MOC/permit correlation. Escalate immediately to process safety and T0800 workflows.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="ics:sis"
| where match(status_message, "(?i)bypass|override|maintenance|manual|inhibit")
| lookup ot_sis_authorized_changes loop_id user OUTPUT permit_id valid_until
| eval permit_ok=if(!isnull(permit_id) AND _time<=valid_until, 1, 0)
| where permit_ok=0
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen values(status_message) as states by loop_id, asset, user
```

Understanding this SPL

**Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Bypass** — Monitors mode changes, jumpering, or overrides on SIS (T0800), directly tied to process safety and regulatory expectations.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:sis"` safety PLC diagnostics, alarm historians. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: ics:sis. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="ics:sis". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(status_message, "(?i)bypass|override|maintenance|manual|inhibit")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **permit_ok** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where permit_ok=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by loop_id, asset, user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare failed or disabled searches in Splunk to the **OT Security Add-on** configuration UI and the **Saved Searches** list so names and schedules line up. Spot-check one failure against `scheduler.log` around the run time.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: State timeline per SIS loop, Alert list with permit linkage, Gauge of open bypasses.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for safety or interlock systems being sidestepped in ways that only happen when someone tries to work around the guardrails.
